title: practical english usage - Part 118
content:
But note that no article is used with nature, society or space when these have a British people talk about the weather a lot. I love listening to the wind.

My wife likes the seaside, but I prefer the mountains.

the weather, the sunshine, the night.

the town, the country, the mountains, the sea, the seaside, the wind, the rain, is familiar with what we  are talking about. Examples are: environment - the world around us and its climate. The suggests that everybody The is used with a number of rather general expressions referring to our physical 4 physical      environment:         the    town,      the    sea African butterflies  the butterflies of Africa 1960s music     the music of the 1960s by a limiting, defining phrase, especially one with of. Compare: we  usually use no article. However, the is often used when the noun is followed grammar «142     special rules and exceptions They've developed a new variety of sheep.                 “ > What kind of (a) person is she?  Have you got a cheaper sort of radio?

(►  592).

We   usually leave out a/an after kind of, sort of, type of and similar expressions 6 kind     of,   etc He's been away all week. I haven't seen her all day.

and all summer.

We usually leave out the after all in all day, all night, all week, all year, all winter All (the) three brothers were arrested.

And we  generally leave out the between all and a number.

Both (the) children are good at languages.

We often leave out the after both.

5 both      and   all a spot on the sun = a sunspot lessons in how to play the guitar = guitar lessons When a noun modifies another noun, the first noun's article is dropped.

the wife of the boss = the boss's wife But the possessive noun itself may have an article.

(     the America's economic problems) the economic problems of America = America's economic problems or the-Jaelds-eoat) not the coat that belongs to Jack = Jack's coat (     Jack's the coat Nouns lose their articles after possessive 's.

3 possessive        's For cases like the bread and (the) butter, ►    276.

from top to bottom inch by inch with hat and coat         arm in arm               husband and wife with knife and fork       on land and sea       day after day Articles are often dropped in double expressions, particularly with prepositions.

2 double      expressions:        with     knife      and     fork Say that again and I'll put you in the hospital.

She was unhappy at the university.

In American English, university and hospital are not used without articles.

I left my coat in the hospital when I was visiting Megan.

- Megan's in hospital, (as a patient) I'll meet you at the college. (The college is just a meeting place.) - I met her at college, (when we  were students) meanings. Compare: With place nouns, expressions with or without articles may have different by radio/phone/letter/mail/email/text by car/bus/bicycle/plane/train/tube/boat on foot by day at night leave/start/enter school/university/college ɑt/from home leave home a/an         the;   my,    your,          this,    that,           Section12 Determiners:          and           etc;        etc grammar •  142   special rules and exceptions He's worked in radio and television all his life.

Cinema is different from theatre in several ways.

art forms or professions.

The is often dropped in all four cases when we  talk about these institutions as What's on TV?

It was a great treat to go to the cinema or the theatre when I was a child. (BrE) I always listen to the radio while I'm driving.

the radio, the cinema (BrE), the theatre, but television or TV.

When  we  talk about our use of these forms of entertainment, we  generally say 11   (the)   radio,      (the)   cinema,        (the)   theatre        and   television She studied oboe and saxophone at the Royal Academy of Music.

This recording was made with Miles Davis on trumpet.

talking about classical music.

But the is often dropped when talking about jazz or pop, and sometimes when The violin is really difficult. Who's that on the piano?
